defaultSeo.title: Blog App Monorepo
defaultSeo.description: A modern monorepo with Next.js apps, Sanity CMS, shared modules, and design tokens.

heros.title: Build faster with a composable monorepo

children.text: This starter combines Next.js 15 apps, Sanity CMS, reusable modules, and a shared design system so teams can move quickly without sacrificing consistency.
What you can build
Use this repository to build production-ready pages, test design ideas, and evolve shared modules used by multiple apps.
It is ideal for teams that want a single source of truth for UI, content schema, and type-safe data contracts.

items.title: Why use a monorepo for this project?

children.text: A monorepo keeps shared code and app code in sync, reducing duplication and integration drift.

items.title: How does content reach the frontend?

children.text: Sanity documents are queried via next-sanity and rendered through a section registry in the blog app.
Yes. Add schema definitions in Studio, implement rendering modules in packages, and reuse them across pages.

items.title: Architecture Map
items.summary: Understand package boundaries and shared dependencies.

title: Monorepo Architecture Explained
excerpt: A practical tour of apps, packages, and data flow inside this Turborepo workspace.

children.text: Why this structure
The repository separates product apps from reusable packages. This improves ownership and reduces coupling.
The blog app focuses on rendering. The studio focuses on authoring. Shared packages hold UI and section modules.

title: Building a Page Builder with Sanity Sections
excerpt: How section schemas and module renderers work together to let editors compose pages safely.

children.text: Section-first content modeling
Each module has a schema and a renderer. Editors choose sections in Studio, and the app resolves each section by type.
This pattern keeps UX flexible while preserving strong technical guardrails.

title: Shared UI and Types Across Apps
excerpt: Design tokens, component packages, and generated content types that scale with your app portfolio.

children.text: One system, many surfaces
Shared design tokens and UI primitives create consistent experiences in blog and docs applications.
Generated types from Sanity close the gap between authored content and frontend rendering contracts.

title: Monorepo Best Practices in 2026
excerpt: Practical patterns for scaling apps, packages, and teams in a single monorepo without slowing delivery.

children.text: Start with clear ownership boundaries
A healthy monorepo is not about putting everything in one place. It is about defining stable package APIs and ownership so teams can ship independently.
Prefer package contracts over cross-app imports
Keep dependency direction simple: apps consume packages, and packages avoid importing app code. This prevents circular coupling and keeps refactors safe.
Invest in task pipelines early
Use cached pipelines for lint, type-check, and build. Teams should get fast feedback locally and consistent outcomes in CI.
Document decisions where contributors work
Architecture docs, conventions, and examples should live near the codebase. This reduces onboarding time and keeps standards practical.

title: Next.js App Router Best Practices
excerpt: How to structure modern Next.js apps with server components, lean client bundles, and predictable data flows.

children.text: Use Server Components by default
Fetch data in Server Components whenever possible and move only interactive islands to Client Components. This keeps bundles smaller and improves first load performance.
Keep route segments focused
Use route groups for organization and colocate route-specific components close to each page. Shared concerns should stay in reusable packages.
Design for loading and failure states
Provide clear loading and not-found paths. Route-level error boundaries and graceful fallbacks make content-driven experiences more resilient.
Optimize media and metadata
Use optimized images, descriptive metadata, and strong semantic structure to improve performance, SEO, and accessibility together.

title: Sanity Content Modeling Best Practices
excerpt: A practical guide to designing flexible schemas, safe editorial workflows, and predictable frontend projections.

children.text: Model for reuse, not just for pages
Create module-style objects that can be reused across home pages, landing pages, and templates. This gives editors flexibility while keeping rendering logic stable.
Validate author input at the schema level
Use required fields, min and max limits, and conditional validation rules for links and references. Better validation reduces broken content in production.
Keep projections explicit
In GROQ queries, project only what the frontend needs and normalize link fields. Explicit projections simplify components and prevent hidden data dependencies.
Treat content models as product APIs
Version and review schema changes carefully. A stable content contract allows teams to iterate quickly without breaking page composition.

title: Monorepo Release Strategy Without Bottlenecks
excerpt: How to keep release cycles independent in a shared repository using package boundaries and CI gates.

children.text: Separate change scope from repo scope
A large repository does not require large releases. Track changed workspaces and run only the necessary validation pipelines for each pull request.
Define quality gates per workspace
Critical packages should enforce stricter checks, while docs-only changes can use lighter validation to keep feedback fast.
Use consistent versioning rules
Adopt a clear strategy for package version updates and changelogs so consumers can understand impact before deployment.

title: Next.js Performance Playbook for Content Sites
excerpt: A practical checklist for Core Web Vitals in App Router projects with CMS-driven content.

children.text: Reduce client JavaScript by default
Use client components only for interactions. Keep layout, data fetching, and rendering-heavy sections on the server.
Optimize image delivery
Provide responsive sizes and avoid oversized hero assets. Prioritize meaningful visuals while preserving fast first render.
Measure and tune real routes
Profile slow pages using production-like data and monitor LCP, CLS, and INP continuously after each release.

title: Sanity Editorial Workflows That Scale
excerpt: Build reliable publishing flows with validation, previews, and clear content responsibilities.

children.text: Design schemas for real editorial tasks
Model content around what editors need to update frequently, and hide technical complexity behind well-structured fields.
Use validation as guardrails
Field-level rules for links, slugs, and required blocks prevent incomplete content from reaching production.
Pair preview with query discipline
Keep GROQ projections explicit and aligned with UI contracts so preview behavior matches published rendering.
